-- 4 of 59 --

My Journey Into Storytelling
Matthew Dicks is the internationally bestselling author of the novels Memoirs
of an Imaginary Friend, Something Missing, Unexpectedly, Milo, The Perfect
Comeback of Caroline Jacobs, Twenty-one Truths About Love, and The Other
Mother, and the nonfiction titles Storyworthy: Engage, Teach, Persuade, and
Change Your Life through the Power of Storytelling and Someday Is Today: 22
Simple Actionable Ways to Propel Your Creative Life.
His novels have been translated into more than 25 languages worldwide.
Matthew is a record 56-time Moth StorySLAM champion and 9-time
GrandSLAM champion who tells stories on stages all over the world.
Matthew’s clients have included Amazon, Slack, Johnson & Johnson, Saatchi
& Saatchi, Salesforce, MetLife, Pfizer, The Freeman Companies, Rustic
Pathways, and many more.
Matthew is the creator, producer, and co-host of Speak Up Storytelling, a
podcast that teaches people to find and tell their best stories.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

Worksheet #1 - Exploring Your Roles
Write your role here…
Do this:
Make a list of all the roles you have in life.
We tend to have many. Some of mine include teacher, author, storyteller,
husband, father, son-in-law, golfer, cyclist, Patriots season ticket holder,
consultant, columnist, Boy Scout volunteer, Little League spectator, blogger,
cat owner (as much as you can own a cat), investor, podcaster, and more.
Give me (or you) enough time and the list will get exceptionally long.
Step #1: What are the roles I have in life?
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 7 of 59 --

Could finding and telling better stories in that particular role make me more
effective?
Could I become a better teacher, husband, investor, son-in-law, Little
League spectator, and all the rest if I found and told better stories?
Now look at each item on that list and ask yourself:
For most of the roles that you fill in life, the answer is probably yes.
Let this expansion of storytelling throughout your life remain everpresent in
your mind as you move through this course, because even though becoming a
better storyteller at a Patriots tailgate or at dinner with my in-laws won’t help
me in any direct way with my business, storytelling is a skill that must be
practiced relentlessly to get better.
If you reserve your storytelling and the lessons taught in this course for
business purposes only, you’re missing out on important opportunities to
practice as well as even more important opportunities to forge more meaningful
connections with the world.
Step #2: Follow Up Questions
Don’t just become a better storyteller for business.
Become a better storyteller in your life.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 8 of 59 --

The Economics of Storytelling:
Leveraging Pre-existing Skills
Storytelling improves communication.
Storytelling can establish surprising connections.
Storytelling can allow for benevolent coercion.
Storytelling can create a supportive community
Storytelling can allow for personal understanding.
Storytelling can promote authentic healing.
Storytelling makes things memorable.
Storytelling makes things exciting.
Storytelling makes things emotionally appealing.
One of the best things about storytelling is the return on investment. Storytelling
doesn't cost a lot because we leverage skills and experience that you and your
employees already possess. You've been telling stories for your entire life. We will
simply level up a skill that you already possess.
Let’s talk about some specific examples of how a story will remain in the hearts and
minds of an audience and how storytelling can transform an idea into a concept that
cannot be ignored.
Sometimes storytelling requires daring. It requires a willingness to do something that
others are not. Injecting storytelling, a personal anecdote, and even some personal
vulnerability can transform the ordinary into the extraordinary, but it’s easier said
than done. Examples of its effectiveness are important. The proof, they say, is in the
pudding. Here is some pudding.
It’s hard to dive off a high board without checking to see if there is water in the pool.
It’s hard to dive off the board without watching someone dive first. It’s hard to dive
off the high board even after watching someone dive off.
Even after watching a child dive off.
What you must know is that you’ve done hard things before. You’ve done everything
in your life for the first time, so this version of storytelling - the righteous version - is
exactly the same.
It simply requires enough daring and courage to do it once.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 9 of 59 --

What ideas, concepts, or lessons do you remember because they’ve been tied to
story?
What books or movies have changed the way you look at the yourself or the world?
What stories do you want to hear again even though you’ve heard them so many
times before?
What movie dialogue have you memorized?
What stories have kept you awake at night?
Make a list of the reasons why you might be be afraid to attempt this version of
storytelling.
Once the list is made, destroy it. The late author, Howard Frank Mosher, would affix bad
reviews to the side of his barn and short them with a shotgun.
My good friend, Dan, is fond of buring things that hold him back. Sometimes these are
worries written on a slip of paper. Sometimes they are his 2-iron or the photograph of a
particularly annoying person.
Destroy your fears in the best, most memorable, and safest way you see fit.
Also, doing so will give you a story to tell.
Remember:
Stories are memorable. The authenticity, vulnerability, and narrative of the story can
make ideas incredibly sticky in the minds of others?
Has a PowerPoint deck, a graph or chart, or a lecture ever done any of these things for
you?
Employee Buy-In:
Why People Love Storytelling
Little will need to be done to garner employee buy-in for storytelling, simply because
storytelling has universal appeal. The work that we do to improve your business can also
be used in your employees' personal life, too. When employees see this link between
professional and personal growth, their willing engagement in storytelling increases
exponentially.
Unlike a lot of training and professional development, employee buy-in is huge because
it's immediately and personally applicable
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 10 of 59 --

Companies spend enormous amounts of money attempting to motivate, inspire, retain,
and level up employees. Many of these attempts are temporary, fleeting, or ineffective.
Storytelling avoids all of that by making the professional also personal.
Selling storytelling instruction to your people will not be hard when framed properly.
Storytelling instruction will create a more connected community of employees, which
will increase collaboration and retention.
Storytelling is a unique skill that can be learned at work but willingly and enthusiastically
practised at home.
Employers want their people to be happy, productive, and fulfilled. In an attempt to
make this possible, we offer coffee, yoga rooms, extra vacation days, and higher salaries.
Sometimes these things work, but they don’t improve performance. Storytelling teaches
employees how to be more interesting, engaging, self actualized human beings while also
making them more profitable for your business. When you offer that training to them,
you get all the credit.
Return on investment is critical to the success of a business, yet so often, the
investments we make in professional development are fleeting and unrealized. You
spend a day learning a new skill, then your people return to work, catching up on emails
that they missed during the training and failing to transfer the learning to action.
Storytelling is so personally applicable that your employees will be more likely to engage
in the training simply because it is personally as well as professionally appealing. It is
also easily integrated into your day to day processes and routines, making it a part of
your culture rather than a fly-by-night activity. Sell the training and motivate your
employees by listing the personal benefits of storytelling alongside the professional
ones.
As you prepare to introduce storytelling to your organization, be sure to think about the
ways in which you will pitch this process to your people.
For parents, you can talk about how storytelling improves communication with children
and helps to teach more memorable lessons.
For singletons, you can talk about how storytelling can improve their dating life.
For career climbers, you can talk about how storytelling helps in presentations, pitch
meetings, interviews, and more.
Make a list of the kinds of people who work at your company, and then consider how
storytelling might help them on a personal level.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 11 of 59 --

Zig vs. Zag:
Dare to Be Different
Storytelling asks you to do something different
than your competitors. You have a choice in the
way that you communicate:
You can follow along with industry expectations or
you can blaze your own unique trail.
It seems fairly obvious that the letter produces far
better results, yet fear often prevents people from
choosing the latter.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

Worksheet #2 - Zig vs. Zag - Dare to be Different
Then look at your list. Consider each person carefully. Rate the person along the scale
of conformity, with a 1 being the ultimate conformist and a 10 being the connumate
rebel.
Did the people on your list lead a life of expectation and conformity, or did they blaze
new trails, take great risks, step outside their comfort zones, and zig when others zag?
I suspect most, if not all, of the names on your lean toward the rebel side of the
ledger.
Most, and probably all, were zaggers.
If the people who you admire most were zaggers, why not you?
Try this:
Make a list of 10 people, living or dead, who you admire. The list could include
friends, colleagues, courageous explorers, famous scientists, clergy members, test
pilots, New England Patriots, entrepreneurs…
Take some time. List more than 10 if you’d like.

-- 13 of 59 --

Professional AND Personal:
Why Both Elements are Key
Practice is imperative in order to improve your storytelling skills. This is why it's
important to bring this instruction to your personal AND professional life.
Also, the personal stories that we tell can often be used in business settings to great
effect. Only through this practice and expansion of the personal into the
professional will you become a highly effective storyteller.
I know you’ve come to this course looking to learn storytelling for business.
But let’s reframe that a bit:
You’ve come to this course looking to become a better storyteller.
There is no such thing as a "good business storyteller" and a "good personal
storyteller".
There are simply "GOOD STORYTELLERS"
Notes:
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 15 of 59 --

Homework for Life
The best storytellers can answer questions, provide examples, construct arguments,
convince others, and support assertions through stories. The more stories you have,
the more successful you will be, so finding stories is crucial. Let me tell you how.
There is nothing more important in this course than Homework for Life.
It is the foundation upon which you will begin finding stories, seeing stories in your
everyday life, and discovering how infinitesimal and unforgettable a story can be.
Seeing and telling stories from your everyday life will change the way you communicate
with others.
It is the difference between being a storyteller with a handful of great stories and a
storyteller who always has something new, entertaining, and engaging to say.

-- 17 of 59 --

Did someone say something today that made you laugh? Cry? Become angry? Feel
confused?
Did anyone’s words make you see them or yourself or something else in a brand new light?
Did you do something today that you’ve never done before? Do something in a way you’ve
never done it before?
Did you watch someone do something you’ve never done before?
Did you fail at something today? Succeed at something? Come agonizingly close to success?
Did you see something that you’ve never seen before? Rarely seen? Did you see something
that you see almost every day but in a new light today?
Did you think a new thought today? Ask yourself a question? Answer a question? Find the
wrong answer to a question?
Did a memorable “What if?” cross your mind today?
Did you lose hope today? Find hope? Wish for hope?
Did you say something today that you regret? Did you say something that you liked? Did you
want to say something but couldn’t find the courage to do so? Did you think of the perfect
thing to say after the fact?
Did you remember a moment from your past? Something you’d forgotten or rarely think
about?
Did you do something that you would never tell anyone about? Did you think something that
you would never tell anyone about?
Did you do or think something that you wish you could tell someone about?
Did you keep a secret? Uncover a secret? Wish something had remained a secret?
Did something make you happy today? Sad? Embarrassed? Humiliated? Frightened?
Nervous? Less confident? More confident?
Did you want to punch someone today? Hug someone?
Did you have the urge to do something today but fail to find the courage to do so?
Did you end the day with a regret?

First Last Best Worst
We move through our lives failing to see, tell, and record some of our most meaningful
stories. But those stories still exist, just waiting for us to find, tell, and record them.
First Last Best Worst is one way of finding the stories you've left behind and expanding
the view of your life as a whole.
WORKSHEET #4 - First Last Best Worst

-- 19 of 59 --

Where Oh Where
One of the best ways of finding a story is to take a mental tour of the locations of our
lives.
Stories hide in places, so when we return to those places, we can often find stories
that we have left behind. "Where Oh Where" teaches you how to do this.
Here is a list of locations to start. For each location, make a list of all the locations
where you have actually spent time in your life. All the zoos, ferris wheels, and
puddles that you can recall.
Then place yourself in each of those places in your mind’s eye and see what you can
see, hear, and remember. If you give yourself a chance, there is a good possibility
that you will remember something previously forgotten.
Where Oh Where Locations

-- 20 of 59 --

3-2-1
Choose 3 tangible, non proper nouns. You can use the online generator or ask for
prompts from the people playing with you (thus the #3).
Choose one of those prompts. Tell a story from your life that includes one of
these 3 prompts. The story can be as short as 20 seconds and as long as 2 minute
(thus the #2).
You have 1 minute to prepare the story (thus the #1).
1.
2.
3.
The purpose of this exercise is not to tell an outstanding storiy but to find new
stories. This is why you only have one minute to prepare.
Low stakes. No expectations. Just find stories.
3-2-1 is a storytelling improv game that invites you to look through your life through
the lens of a single object. Oftentimes a pinpoint, laser-pointer like prompt through
your life can yield stories previously forgotten.
3-2-1 also affords you the opportunity to practice the storytelling strategies you will
be learning in coming lessons.
It’s also an excellent game to play at the beginning of a meeting or as a break in
conference setting. It affords your people the opportunity to tell stories absent any
expectations and stakes and promotes greater connection and trust in teams.
You can use this online object generator to produce the three words that you will
use for your prompts.
https://perchance.org/object
Rules for 3-2-1
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

"Why do you do the things you do?"
As you move through the next few days, examine how you live your life. Ask yourself
these questions:
Storytellers are deeply curious about themselves, and through this curiosity, great
stories can be found. This lesson teaches you how to sharpen that curiosity so you
can find the best stories possible.
Daily Practice #2 - Why do you do the things you do?
1. What do you do that you don’t tell others about?
2. What do you conceal from friends or loved ones?
3. What makes you anxious or nervous?
4. What makes you frightened?
5. What brings you shame?
6.Look for habits and ask yourself, “How did this habit originate?
7. When do you pretend to be something you’re not?
8. What moments in the day are especially hard for you?
9. What moments in the day bring you joy?
10. When is your response to others surprising to you?
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 23 of 59 --

You’re delivering an all-hands, a keynote, or a speech
to stakeholders. One of the best ways to connect to
an audience, gain their trust, inspire them, and be
entertaining is to weave personal stories into the
content.
Your marketing deck calls for a metaphor to
accurately explain a complex process, an evolving
market, or a complicated new product. The stories
from your life can ideally become the metaphors that
you need.
You're pitching a business idea to investors. How did
this business evolve? Who are you and why should
investors trust you? How will your background assist
in your success? All of this can (and should) be
conveyed via storytelling.
You’re creating an onboarding video for new
employers. You need to convey new content and also
make them feel like a part of the team. You’ll want to
convey norms, traditions, and culture. All of this can
(and should) be done through storytelling.
Your brand is evolving and you want to convey the
new messaging to prospective and current clients.
This should be done via a story. All of the storytelling
strategies that you use to tell personal stories can be
applied to your brand story, too.
Injecting Storytelling Into Your
Business
How can storytelling help your business specifically? Here are just a few examples.

-- 25 of 59 --

What is a Story?
Or…
This is true if any story of any worth. Once you see it, you can’t unsee it.
One of the best ways to become accustomed to this principle is to look for
it in movies.
The first barrier to telling a great story is understanding what constitutes a
story. This lesson teaches you the difference between a story and everything
that pretends to be a story.
A story is about change. That change usually takes the form of either
transformation or realization.
You were once one kind of person, and then some stuff
happened, and now you are a different kind of person.
You once thought one thing, and then some stuff happened,
and now you think something different.
Story:
"The Blood Draw"
Story:
"The Spoon of Power"
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

WORKSHEET #7 - Find Your Professional Story

Ideas for this story include:
What is the origin story for your company?

-- 29 of 59 --

The Frame of Your Story
What is the thesis of your personal story? What is the moment of transformation or
realization that you want to share?
The beginning and end of the story are "the frame" of the story. It's the first
and most important choice you'll make while crafting a story.
WORKSHEET #8 - Creating the Frame of Your Personal Story
Step #1: Ending Your Story
Begin by asking yourself: What is your 5 second moment? This is the singular
moment in time when the transformation or realization took place.
This is the end of your story.
Step #2: Beginning Your Story
What is the opposite of your 5 second moment? This will become the beginning of
your story.
Step #3: Creating Your Thesis Statement
Now you can complete your thesis statement. This will guide all decisions_that need
to be made in the crafting of your story.
I used to be
but now I am
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 30 of 59 --

WORKSHEET #9 - Creating the Frame of Your Professional or Business Story
What is the thesis of your business story? What is the moment of transformation or
realization that you want to share?
Step #1: Ending Your Story
Begin by asking yourself: What is your 5 second moment? This is the singular
moment in time when the transformation or realization took place.
This is the end of your story.
Step #2: Beginning Your Story
What is the opposite of your 5 second moment? This will become the beginning of
your story.
Step #3: Creating Your Thesis Statement
Now you can complete your thesis statement. This will guide all decisions_that
need to be made in the crafting of your story.
My company/my product/my service/I used to be
but now my company/my product/my service/I am
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 31 of 59 --

Beginnings
Start your story with location & action
Avoid adjectives and use good, hard nouns
Avoid exposition. Do not teach your audience about things in the story until
you’ve started the story.
Start as close to the end as possible
The beginning of any story is precious territory.
It's your chance to grab attention, create wonder, and convince your audience that
you have something entertaining, engaging, and important to say. These strategies
will help make that happen.
Strategies from this lesson:
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 32 of 59 --

Daily Practice #4 - Beginnings
Go back to our list of movies, or better yet, pay attention to the beginning moments
of movies you are watching today and tomorrow and forever more.
Or simply turn on Netflix, choose a movie at random, and watch the first 2-5 minutes.
Do this again and again and again.
Ask yourself:
1. Are these movies adhering to some or all of the strategies above?
2. Can you predict the ending of the movie based upon the beginning of the story?
Not the specificity of events, of course, but what changes the protagonist will
experience by the end of the film.
For example:
Harry and Sally hate each other at the beginning of the movie. By the end of the
movie, they will love each other, because “love” is the opposite of “hate.”
Phil Connors in Groundhog Day hates Groundhog Day, Punxsutawney, most people,
and himself at the beginning of the movie, so by the end, he’s going to love all of
these things.
Clarise Starling in Silence of the Lambs is discounted by the male FBI agents around
her and seems to be lacking in confidence as a result. By the end of the movie, she
will be respected by the FBI agents, possibly grudgingly, but more importantly, she
will find greater respect and self confidence in herself.
Once you train yourself to see these things in film, books, and even television shows,
you will see it more easily in the story of your life, too.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 33 of 59 --

WORKSHEET #10 - Beginning Your Personal Story
Start with the opposite or an approximation of the opposite of the end of your
story.
Start your story with location and action.
Avoid loading the beginning with adjectives. Instead, use good, hard nouns.
Avoid exposition. Do not teach your audience about things in the story until
you’ve started the story.
Start as close to the end as possible
Take the personal story that you have previously chosen and craft a strong
beginning by using as many of the strategies as possible.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 34 of 59 --

WORKSHEET #11 - Beginning Your Professional or Business Story
Start with the opposite or an approximation of the opposite of the end of your
story.
Start your story with location and action.
Avoid loading the beginning with adjectives. Instead, use good, hard nouns.
Avoid exposition. Do not teach your audience about things in the story until
you’ve started the story.
Start as close to the end as possible
Take the personal story that you have previously chosen and craft a strong
beginning by using as many of the strategies as possible. The story may be about
you, your company, your product, or an innovation, but the principles remain the
same.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 35 of 59 --

Endings
SHOW then (if necessary) TELL at the end, to allow the audience the opportunity
to land on the ending first and maintain wonder
End in the heart of the storyteller
Avoid endings that strip the meaning of the story with meaningless humor
Consider carefully what you want to say and what you might want to leave out
Signal to your audience that the story is going to the end using strategies like:
Reduce your volume
Slow the pacing of your speech
Increase the length of pauses
If you’re onstage, move front and center
Landing your story with a satisfying ending is critical.
Your audience has invested time and attention in your story, so they expect an
ending that rewards that investment.
This lesson helps ensure that you end your stories in a way that satisfies your
audience. Your ending should convince our audience that the time they invested
was well spent.
Strategies from this lesson:
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 36 of 59 --

WORKSHEET #12 - Ending Your Personal Story
Craft your ending. What is the moment of transformation that you want to share? If
an audience was asked to summarize your story, what would they say was the
purpose or meaning of your story, because that is what the ending of a story should
be:
The culmination of all that came before.
Questions to help you refine your ending
1. Show vs. State
Can I simply show my transformation or realization or do I also need to state it as
well? The best stories are the ones you can show, but sometimes it’s necessary to
state it as well.
2. Include vs. Exclude
What can I remove from the end of the story that might keep my audience wondering
about long after the story is finished?
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 37 of 59 --

WORKSHEET #13 - Ending Your Professional or Business Story
Craft your ending. What is the moment of transformation that you want to share? If
an audience was asked to summarize your story, what would they say was the
purpose or meaning of your story, because that is what the ending of a story should
be:
The culmination of all that came before.
Questions to help you refine your ending
1. Show vs. State
Professional stories require your to both SHOW IT AND SAY IT. A personal story is
successful even if it’s simply entertaining. If your audience fails to see the moment of
realization or transformation, they can still enjoy your story.
Think Jurassic Park. Even if you don’t see the story within the story, the dinosaurs are
still cool.
But a professional story must land the message, otherwise the story has failed.
Professional stories have a purpose attached to a dollar sign. If the professional
story does not yield profit, it doesn’t matter if it was entertaining.
Therefore, we must always state our ending message in a business story, even if we
think we’ve shown it perfectly. We can’t take chances when the goals are so clear and
so important.
2. Include vs. Exclude
What can I remove from the end of the story that might keep my audience wondering
about long after the story is finished?
In a business story, this is oftentimes an indication about where you or the company
might be going next. It could be a hint or indication about what you or your company
are thinking about next in terms of a product or service. It could be a prediction
about were the market is going or what areas you are looking to innovate next.
Stagnant companies and stagnant people are boring. Customers, clients, investors,
and partners do not get excited about a person or a company that is gong to be
exactly the same tomorrow, so ending the story with the notion of future evolution -
minus specific details - is often the way to go.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 38 of 59 --

See Your Scenes
Telling Your Story in Scenes
….
….
….
Sketch the scene
List characters
Record dialogue or description that you want to remember
Write down transition sentences between scenes
Highlight moments of surprise
Label backstory
What are the main scenes (moments) of your story?
In these comic panes, you can
One of the best ways to guarantee that your stories are structured in scenes is to
make use of comic book panels and storyboarding. This lesson will show you how.
A well-crafted and told story creates a movie in the mind of the audience.These are
the most memorable, most engaging, and most moving stories. The power of
location is a simple but critical principle in this pursuit. It creates the much-desired
movie in thenmind of the audience.

-- 41 of 59 --

The best storytellers say the things that most people are afraid to say.
This is vulnerability, and contrary to the impression of many, it is a sign of courage
and strength.
Vulnerability
One of the best ways to find the courage to be
vulnerable is to begin marking the moments
when others around you are vulnerable.
When we see others doing something and
finding success, and more importantly, not
being ridiculed, attacked, or otherwise treated
poorly, it makes it seem more doable for us.
One of the best examples of the willingness to be publicly vulnerable in the world
is Megan Washington’s TED Talk.
Watch it.
Then start looking for it
in the world.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 42 of 59 --

Module #4:
Emotions:
Emotionally Connecting To Your Audience
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 43 of 59 --

Why You Can't Be Steve Jobs
When Steve Jobs took the stage, he
carried a well-known story on stage with
him.
Chances are you do not. This is why you
must share of yourself whenever you are
speaking to an audience. This lesson will
explain how and why.
How to identify, preserve and enhance surprise
The strategic manipulation of the emotional response of the audience
A demonstration of how the “amazing coincidence” can never be the story
Illustration of circular storytelling
Demonstration of vulnerability
Matty the Dog is an excellent example of the following concepts:

Story: "Matty the Dog"
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 44 of 59 --

Mapping Emotions
Storytellers seek to manipulate their audience's emotions.
In order to do this effectively, a storyteller must map out the desired emotions of the
audience at every step of the journey.
It’s also important to remember how varied emotions can be. The varying degrees of
happy, sad, and angry are important. Stories must rung true to an audience, and
emotional truth is critical.
Understanding the layers of emotions will also help you understand yourself and the
moment you are telling better and assist in the creation of the emotional map of your
story on your Anatomy of a Story worksheet.
This lesson will show you how.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 45 of 59 --

WORKSHEET #14 - Mapping Emotions for Your Personal Story
Frightened but excited
Frustrated but empathetic
Amamored but adored
Color or label your comic book scenes or Anatomy of a Story worksheet according
to the desired emotional response of your audience.
Remember that this is an emotional map of how you want the audience to feel. This
is often exactly the same as how you also felt, but not always.
Remember that we oftentimes feel more than one thing at the same time, and that can
be reality interesting in a story.
For example:
Strategies from this lesson:
For example, there are times in stories when your audience knows about your bad
decision before you did in the moment you’re describing. This is often fun for an
audience, as they will foresee the possible trouble that awaits you. It’s also an excellent
generator of humor. So in a story like this, you may have been feeling completely
confident and utterly in control while the audience understands your incompetence
and is worried on your behalf.
In these cases, the audience’s emotional response will be different than the emotions
you are describing, which is why it’s critical to map the emotions of your story based
upon the desired response of the audience instead of your own response.
Get it?
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 46 of 59 --

WORKSHEET #15 - Mapping Emotions for Your Professional or
Business Story
Here is our product. This is what it does. It does things that you might want.
Here is our service. This is what we do. We do things that you might need.
Here is our company. This is what we do. You might like the things we do.
Remember that when it comes to a business story, it’s critical to know exactly how you
want your audience to feel at every moment of the story, because it’s feelings that will
help determine how your customers, clients, and investors feel about your company,
employees, brand, product, and service.
For this reason, you must first create a plan for how you want your audience to feel and
when you want those feelings to change before you ever consider the content of the
story.
Emotions first. Content second. What you do is never as important as how others feel
about what you do.
I know that might sound annoying given the amount of time, energy, and investment
that you’ve made in your company and its offerings, but it’s true. You product or
service means nothing if your potential customers don’t have an emotional attachment
to it.
Telling a story without planning an emotional map amounts to little more than
reporting the news:
How your audience feels about your company, product, or service, both in the abstract
and how it relates to the world, will almost always determine your success in terms of
sales.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 47 of 59 --

Surprise
Identify all of the potential surprises in the story. Every story has them.
Determine the information that the audience requires in order for the surprise to
pay off. What do you need to teach the audience in order for them to be surprised?
Preserve and protect that information by hiding it from the audience-via the
following strategies:
Give the information a secondary reason for being included in the story (if that
reason doesn’t already exist)
Hide it in a list of other things to minimize its importance
Make it funny
Determine the order or words that maximizes the surprise
In order to maximize surprise in your personal story, follow these steps:
1.
2.
3.
Surprise is almost always the reason an audience laughs, cries, or feels any other
meaningful emotion. It’s why it’s so critical to get it right.
Once you’ve been surprised, it’s hard to remember being surprised, so you forget
that your audience should be surprised.
Surprises are often predicated on providing the audience some just enough
information for the surprise to pay off. Storytellers often provide too much
information or fail to marginalize the information needed so that the audience sees
the surprise long before it arrives.
Surprise requires planning. It’s often predicated on the order of words that you
speak. This level of planning is often ignored by storytellers.
Surprise is the most delightful thing that a storyteller has to offer their audience. It's
unforgettable and often produces the largest emotional responses.
Surprise is also the part of stories that storytellers most frequently botch. The
problems are many:
1.
2.
3.
This lesson will help you identify, preserve, and enhance surprise in the stories you tell.
WORKSHEET #16 - Creating Surprise in Your Personal Story
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 48 of 59 --

WORKSHEET #17 - Creating Surprise in Your Professional or Business Story
Identify all of the potential surprises in the story. Every story has them.
Determine the information that the audience requires in order for the surprise ro
pay off. What do you need to teach the audience in order for them to be surprised?
Preserve and protect that information by hiding it from the audience via the
following strategies:
Give the information a secondary reason for being included in the story (if that
reason doesn’t already exist)
Hide it in a list of other things to minimize its importance
Make it funny
Determine the order or words that maximizes the surprise
Surprise is often discounted in professional and business settings, but it is just as
important and impactful. A surprise in business can amount to the reveal of a new
product or feature, the pivot of a company from one strategy to another, the
announcement of a new market or location, a change in price point, a technical
innovation, a personal story contained in a larger, more corporate story, or more.
In order to maximize surprise in your professional personal story, the follow steps still
apply:
1.
2.
3.
Surprise is almost always the reason an audience laughs, cries, or feels any other
meaningful emotion. It’s why it’s so critical to get it right.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 49 of 59 --

Daily Practice #5 - Surprise
When was I surprised?
How was I surprised?
What did the storyteller do to trick me to think one thing and then the other?
What information did I need to allow the surprise to happen?
What didn’t I know? What did the storyteller hold back?
How did the storyteller conceal that information so I didn’t recognize its
importance?
How did they make that surprise pay off?
One of the best ways to improve your ability to surprise your audiences is to find
instructive surprises in your daily life.
When you're watching a movie, commercial, or reading a book, look for moments
wherein you were surprised. Ask yourself what strategies were used to create the
surprise.
The more often you can see and diagnose the surprise, the better you will be at doing
the same for your audiences.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 50 of 59 --

What Should You Share?
The Subtle Art of Sharing Oneself
Are you married?
Are you in a committed relationship?
What does your spouse/partner do for a living?
Do you have children?
Do you have pets?
Where do you live?
Where did you grow up?
Where have you lived before?
Are you close to or care for your parents?
Do you watch or play any sports?
What are your hobbies?
Do you like to cook, and if so, what?
Do you play a musical instrument?
Do you have a favorite kind of music or a favorite musical group?
Are you in the process of a home project or renovation?
Do you have any side hustles?
Did you have jobs outside your current job?
What other places have you worked?
What are you currently reading?
Are you the member of a church or religious institution?
Do you volunteer for any recognizable organizations?
Where did you go to college?
Making a list of the kinds of things that you should share with people will help you be
prepared when the time comes. This lesson will help you create that inventory.
The more we share with people, the more likely they will connect and trust us.
Storytelling is a highly effective way of doing this, but so, too, is the more subtle art
of sharing bits of yourself in everyday conversation. This lesson shows you how.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 51 of 59 --

Module #5:
Perspective:
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 52 of 59 --

Perspective:
When, Why, and How?
Third Person
Second Person
The first person is the default storytelling perspective. It is a story about yourself,
using “I” as the primary pronoun. It’s how you tell almost all of the stories in your
life.
The second person is a perspective rarely used in everyday storytelling but can be
enormously powerful when deployed well. It uses “you” as the primary pronoun,
meaning that you are effectively placing the audience in the position of protagonist.
Learn how and when in this lesson.
The choice of perspective is an important one in storytelling. The first, second, and
third person each offer specific strengths and weaknesses for the storyteller. Learn
how to make the right choice in this lesson.
Third Person
Storytelling in the third person is usually the
least preferred method of storytelling, but it's
also necessary at times. It’ uses the pronouns
“he,” “she,” “it,” and “they.” It is equivalent to
taking a bird’s eye view of a story.
Learn how and when in this lesson.
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 53 of 59 --

Module #6
Humor:
2
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 54 of 59 --

Finding Humor in the World
In order to become more effective at deploying humor, you must both see humor
in the world and practice whenever possible.
To this end, do the following:
1 - Whenever you are watching a movie, take note of just one time when you
laughed. Ask yourself, “What about that moment was funny?
Seeing and dissecting humor in real life is a quick and powerful way to deploy the
same strategies yourself. You’ll be shocked to discover how many times the same
strategy is deployed again and again and again.
It could be the result of one of the five strategies included here, or it could be
something entirely different. The five included in the course are just a fraction of
the strategies that I teach. Maybe you can find some of your own.
2 - Whenever you are watching a television show, do the same thing. Take note of
just one time when you laughed. Ask yourself, “What about that moment was
funny?
3 - When you laugh at something in the wild - a friend or family member makes you
laugh, you hear a joke during a marketing pitch or keynote that causes a chuckle (as
improbable as I know that might be), do the same. Ask yourself, “Why was that
moment funny?”
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 56 of 59 --

Module #7:
The Curse of Knowledge:
2
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 57 of 59 --

The Curse of Knowledge
Cardigan V. Barkley
Two commercials with two entirely different results.
Both are effective, of course, but one commercial
does not require prior knowledge, cultural
awareness, or even biographical awareness.
The goal for all communication of any kind should be
to include as many people as possible in the content.
As you tell the stories, ask youself:
Is this a Barkley story or a Cardigan story?
Storytellers need to be keenly aware of what they know so that their audiences do
not feel disconnected from the story. This means addressing issues like
vocabulary, areas of expertise, technical know-how, jargon, acronyms, and more.
Here’s a good rule of thumb:
Describe all things to someone who has never heard of your company, sector, or
line of work. You’re speaking to a fairly intelligent, completely ignorant person.
Avoid anything that might not be understood.
Create a list your “blind spots” here:
Copyright © 2022 Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks
